refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of load→travel→dump cycle time performance against customer-defined targets.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure and report cycle time data, and (2) the operational achievement of a target—the latter is a performance outcome dependent on multiple system and operational factors beyond a single design parameter. By refining to focus on the measurement and reporting capability, the FR becomes solution-neutral (avoids prescribing how cycle time is optimized) and atomic (single, testable obligation). Achievement of the cycle time target is an acceptance criterion validated through operational testing, not a functional requirement of the system design itself.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution captures and reports cycle time metrics (load start → travel start → dump start → dump end timestamps) with ≤5% measurement error under representative duty cycles. Validate that reported p95 cycle time can be compared against customer-defined CT_target.
sub_frs: Capture cycle phase transition events (load initiation, travel initiation, dump initiation, dump completion) with timestamp and location data.
Aggregate and compute cycle time statistics (mean, p95, p99) from captured events.
Report cycle time performance data to Site Manager and System Engineer with sufficient granularity to diagnose deviations from CT_target.
refined_fr.note: The refined FR is solution-neutral but decomposes into three atomic sub-FRs, each addressing a distinct functional obligation in the measurement and reporting pipeline. The acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ CT_target under duty cycle) remains a performance validation gate, not a functional requirement.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument load-bearing structure and dump mechanism with displacement/force transducers; aggregate raw signals into a time-series event log; apply threshold-crossing logic to detect cycle phase boundaries (load pickup, travel start, dump completion); compute elapsed time between phase transitions; compare p95 aggregate against CT_target via statistical analysis module.

DP2: Deploy discrete event sensors at fixed cycle checkpoints (load-ready, travel-start, dump-start, dump-complete); timestamp each event with synchronized clock; reconstruct cycle duration from event sequence; filter outliers using robust statistical methods; report p95 against CT_target.

DP3: Embed cycle-phase inference logic within equipment control system; leverage existing actuator command history and position feedback to infer phase transitions without additional instrumentation; log inferred events with control-system timestamps; compute cycle metrics from inferred timeline; validate against CT_target.

DP4: Hybrid approach—use minimal instrumentation (e.g., dump-gate position + load-cell confirmation) to anchor phase detection; supplement with control-system state machine to infer intermediate phases; fuse both signals for robust event timing; compute cycle duration and p95 performance metric.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4: Hybrid instrumentation and control-system fusion—deploy targeted transducers (load confirmation, dump-gate position) at critical phase boundaries; integrate phase-inference logic into equipment control system to leverage existing actuator feedback; fuse sensor and inferred-state signals to establish robust, timestamped cycle-phase events; compute cycle duration from phase transitions; apply statistical filtering (outlier removal, p95 aggregation) to measured cycle times; compare against CT_target and report performance.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between phase-detection logic and control-system firmware—mitigated by defining a clear event-emission interface (timestamp, phase ID, confidence) that decouples inference from downstream analytics.

Dependency on control-system clock synchronization—acceptable if control system provides monotonic, calibrated timestamps; risk if clock drift exceeds measurement precision (recommend periodic sync or external time reference).
Sensor placement and calibration tied to specific equipment geometry—mitigated by parameterizing threshold values and phase-transition rules; allows reuse across similar duty cycles without redesign.
Statistical analysis module must handle variable cycle counts and missing events—addressed by explicit outlier-detection and data-quality checks before p95 computation.
coupling_check.notes: DP4 balances instrumentation cost (fewer dedicated sensors than DP1/DP2) against inference robustness (leverages control-system state, reducing false positives). Coupling is manageable if phase-event interface is clearly specified and control-system integration is treated as a distinct IM boundary.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Control system provides reliable, timestamped state transitions (actuator commands, position feedback). Risk: If control system lacks precision timing or state logging, phase inference becomes unreliable. Mitigation: Validate control-system timing accuracy during commissioning; add external time reference if drift exceeds ±100 ms.

Assumption: Load-cell and dump-gate sensors are calibrated and stable over duty cycle. Risk: Sensor drift or noise could cause false phase transitions. Mitigation: Implement sensor health monitoring; define confidence thresholds for phase detection.

Assumption: Cycle phases are well-defined and repeatable under agreed duty cycle. Risk: Highly variable or overlapping phases (e.g., partial loads, interrupted dumps) could confound phase detection. Mitigation: Refine phase definitions with customer; document edge cases in operational policy.

Assumption: p95 metric is computed over a sufficiently large sample (e.g., ≥30 cycles per reporting period). Risk: Small sample sizes lead to unstable p95 estimates. Mitigation: Define minimum sample-size requirement in acceptance criteria.

Assumption: Site conditions (temperature, vibration, electromagnetic interference) do not degrade sensor or control-system performance. Risk: Harsh worksite environment could introduce measurement noise or timing jitter. Mitigation: Specify sensor and control-system environmental ratings; validate in representative site conditions.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): Enable measurement and reporting of load→travel→dump cycle time performance, with p95 cycle time ≤ CT_target, by fusing targeted instrumentation (load confirmation, dump-gate position) with control-system state inference; compute cycle duration from timestamped phase-transition events; apply statistical filtering and outlier removal; report p95 aggregate against customer-defined target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions. Acceptance criteria: (1) Phase-detection accuracy ≥95% (validated against manual observation); (2) Measurement precision ±100 ms or better; (3) p95 computation based on ≥30 cycles per reporting period; (4) Sensor and control-system performance validated under site environmental conditions.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized hybrid instrumentation and control-system fusion module that integrates targeted transducers, phase-inference logic, sensor fusion, cycle-time computation, statistical filtering, and performance comparison against cycle-time targets.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmit timestamped cycle-phase events, computed cycle durations, filtered statistics (p95 aggregation), and performance metrics (actual vs. target CT) to external monitoring and analytics systems.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive cycle-time targets (CT_target), instrumentation configuration directives, and control-system integration parameters from supervisory planning and scheduling layer.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emit safety-critical alerts if phase-inference logic detects anomalies, sensor fusion fails, or cycle-time performance deviates significantly from target, triggering safety-service intervention.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication with Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to receive raw actuator feedback, load-confirmation signals, and dump-gate position data; transmit fused phase events and control directives.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment. NGHE Solution is the logical home for hybrid instrumentation fusion, phase-inference logic, sensor fusion algorithms, statistical filtering, and performance comparison. All core DP logic (transducer integration, phase-boundary detection, cycle-time computation, outlier removal, p95 aggregation, CT_target comparison) naturally resides here. Minor diffusion: raw sensor data originates from Vehicle; control directives flow to Vehicle/Teleoperation via Network Link; safety alerts route through SafetyAlert interface.

Poor containment. Vehicle is the source of raw transducer signals (load confirmation, dump-gate position) and actuator feedback, but it should not own the fusion logic, phase inference, statistical filtering, or performance comparison. These are supervisory/analytical functions that belong at the NGHE Solution layer. Placing DP here would scatter control-system integration and analytics across the vehicle boundary, creating tight coupling and poor separation of concerns.

Very poor containment. Teleoperation is a human-in-the-loop interface layer, not an instrumentation or control-fusion platform. Phase-inference logic, sensor fusion, cycle-time computation, and statistical filtering are not teleoperation concerns. Placing DP here would conflate remote operation with autonomous analytics and create severe architectural incoherence.

Very poor containment. Safety Services is responsible for safety-critical monitoring and intervention, not for instrumentation fusion or cycle-time analytics. While Safety Services will consume SafetyAlert outputs from the DP, it should not own the underlying fusion, filtering, or performance-comparison logic. Placing DP here would blur the boundary between safety monitoring and operational analytics.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the optimal module boundary because it is the natural locus for supervisory control logic, sensor fusion, state inference, and performance analytics. The DP's core functions—hybrid instrumentation integration, phase-inference logic, sensor fusion, cycle-time computation, statistical filtering, and CT_target comparison—are all analytical and control-coordination tasks that belong at the solution/orchestration layer, not at the vehicle, teleoperation, or safety-service boundaries. NGHE Solution will consume raw transducer and actuator-feedback signals from Vehicle via Network Link, execute all fusion and filtering logic internally, and emit timestamped phase events, performance metrics, and safety alerts via Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces.
containment_risks: Risk: Raw transducer signals (load confirmation, dump-gate position) originate from Vehicle. If Vehicle hardware or firmware changes, transducer interfaces must be updated in NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Define stable, versioned transducer data contracts via Network Link interface.

Risk: Phase-inference logic depends on equipment control-system feedback (actuator state). If control-system architecture evolves, inference logic may require recalibration. Mitigation: Encapsulate inference logic in a pluggable module within NGHE Solution to allow algorithm updates without affecting core fusion framework.

Risk: Statistical filtering (outlier removal, p95 aggregation) requires tuning parameters (e.g., outlier thresholds, window sizes). If these parameters are hardcoded, NGHE Solution becomes brittle. Mitigation: Externalize filtering parameters via Tasking interface to allow dynamic reconfiguration.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface must be reliable and low-latency. If NGHE Solution's fusion logic is slow or unreliable, safety alerts may be delayed. Mitigation: Implement deterministic, bounded-latency fusion and filtering algorithms; add watchdog timers to detect fusion failures.

Risk: Telemetry interface may become a bottleneck if cycle-phase events and performance metrics are emitted at high frequency. Mitigation: Implement buffering and aggregation in NGHE Solution to reduce telemetry traffic without losing fidelity.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR requirement 'Enable measurement and reporting of load→travel→dump cycle time performance against customer-defined targets' is directly verifiable through the IM boundary (NGHE Solution). Acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions' maps cleanly to NGHE Solution's statistical filtering (p95 aggregation) and CT_target comparison logic. Telemetry interface provides observable outputs (computed cycle durations, filtered statistics, performance metrics) that can be validated against ground truth. Phase-inference logic can be validated through timestamped phase-event logs. Worksite safety plan integration is addressable via SafetyAlert interface.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. The IM boundary (NGHE Solution) is unambiguous: it owns all core DP logic (transducer integration, phase inference, sensor fusion, cycle-time computation, statistical filtering, performance comparison). External interfaces are well-defined: Tasking (inbound CT_target and configuration), Telemetry (outbound metrics), SafetyAlert (outbound anomalies), Network Link (bidirectional raw signals). Ownership is clear; no ambiguity about which module executes which DP function.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Phase-inference logic depends on real-time actuator feedback from Vehicle control system. If Vehicle firmware or actuator interface changes, inference calibration may drift. Mitigation: Define stable actuator-feedback data contracts via Network Link; version inference algorithms independently; implement adaptive recalibration within NGHE Solution.

Risk: Raw transducer signals (load confirmation, dump-gate position) originate from Vehicle hardware. Sensor degradation, calibration drift, or hardware replacement could invalidate fusion assumptions. Mitigation: Implement sensor health monitoring within NGHE Solution; expose sensor-quality metrics via Telemetry; allow dynamic sensor-fusion parameter adjustment via Tasking interface.

Risk: Statistical filtering parameters (outlier thresholds, p95 window size) are currently unspecified. If hardcoded, NGHE Solution becomes brittle to duty-cycle variations or site-condition changes. Mitigation: Externalize all filtering parameters via Tasking interface; document parameter sensitivity; provide tuning guidance in operational procedures.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface must guarantee low-latency, deterministic delivery. If NGHE Solution's fusion pipeline introduces variable latency, safety alerts may be delayed, violating worksite safety plan requirements. Mitigation: Implement bounded-latency fusion algorithms; add watchdog timers; define maximum alert-emission latency in SafetyAlert interface contract.

Risk: Telemetry interface may become a bottleneck if phase-event frequency is high (e.g., 100+ events/cycle). Uncontrolled telemetry traffic could saturate network or overwhelm downstream analytics. Mitigation: Implement event buffering and aggregation in NGHE Solution; define telemetry rate limits; compress or summarize phase events before transmission.

Risk: Phase-inference logic is a novel algorithmic component. If inference fails (e.g., ambiguous phase transitions, sensor noise), cycle-time computation becomes unreliable. Mitigation: Implement inference-confidence scoring; emit low-confidence cycles as anomalies via SafetyAlert; provide fallback inference strategies (e.g., timeout-based phase detection).
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). NGHE Solution is the optimal IM boundary for this DP. All core logic—transducer integration, phase-inference, sensor fusion, cycle-time computation, statistical filtering, and CT_target comparison—naturally resides here as supervisory/analytical functions. Minor diffusion is unavoidable and well-managed: raw sensor data originates from Vehicle (source), control directives flow to Vehicle/Teleoperation (actuators), and safety alerts route through SafetyAlert (safety layer). These are data flows, not logic diffusion. Vehicle (0.35 containment) and Teleoperation (0.28 containment) are correctly excluded from DP ownership; placing fusion logic there would scatter analytics across vehicle and human-interface boundaries, violating separation of concerns. Safety Services (0.22 containment) correctly consumes SafetyAlert outputs but does not own fusion logic. IM boundary is architecturally sound.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 (Hybrid instrumentation and control-system fusion) directly and completely addresses FR (Enable measurement and reporting of load→travel→dump cycle time performance against customer-defined targets). DP logic flow: (1) Deploy targeted transducers at phase boundaries (load confirmation, dump-gate position) → captures critical state transitions. (2) Integrate phase-inference logic into control system → infers cycle phases from actuator feedback. (3) Fuse sensor and inferred-state signals → establishes robust, timestamped phase events. (4) Compute cycle duration from phase transitions → derives cycle time. (5) Apply statistical filtering (outlier removal, p95 aggregation) → produces robust performance metric. (6) Compare against CT_target and report → delivers performance measurement and reporting. Acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ CT_target' is directly satisfied by step 5 (p95 aggregation) and step 6 (CT_target comparison). DP is complete, logically sound, and verifiable. No gaps or logical inconsistencies detected.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Formalize transducer data contracts. Define stable, versioned schemas for load-confirmation and dump-gate-position signals via Network Link interface. Include signal ranges, update frequencies, latency bounds, and failure modes. This mitigates risk of Vehicle hardware changes breaking NGHE Solution.

Revision 2: Externalize statistical filtering parameters. Move outlier-threshold, p95-window-size, and other tuning parameters from hardcoded constants to Tasking interface inputs. Document parameter sensitivity and provide site-specific tuning guidance. This enables adaptation to duty-cycle and site-condition variations.

Revision 3: Define phase-inference confidence scoring. Specify how NGHE Solution quantifies inference confidence (e.g., 0–100 score based on sensor agreement, actuator-feedback consistency). Emit low-confidence cycles as anomalies via SafetyAlert. This improves robustness to sensor noise and ambiguous phase transitions.

Revision 4: Specify SafetyAlert latency bounds. Define maximum time from phase-transition detection to SafetyAlert emission (e.g., ≤100 ms). Implement deterministic, bounded-latency fusion algorithms and watchdog timers. This ensures safety-plan compliance.

Revision 5: Implement telemetry rate limiting. Define maximum phase-event emission rate (e.g., 10 events/sec) and aggregation strategy (e.g., buffer and emit summary statistics every 5 sec). This prevents network saturation while maintaining fidelity.

Revision 6: Encapsulate phase-inference logic as pluggable module. Design inference algorithm as a replaceable component within NGHE Solution to allow algorithm updates (e.g., machine-learning models) without affecting core fusion framework. This supports future evolution.

Revision 7: Add sensor health monitoring. Implement continuous validation of transducer signals (e.g., range checks, rate-of-change limits, cross-sensor consistency). Expose sensor-quality metrics via Telemetry and emit degradation alerts via SafetyAlert. This detects hardware faults early.

Revision 8: Document fallback inference strategies. Specify how NGHE Solution handles inference failures (e.g., sensor loss, ambiguous phase transitions). Define timeout-based fallback phase detection and graceful degradation modes. This improves operational resilience.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, indicating the current FR and DP are logically sound and architecturally well-allocated. However, the gate analysis identifies 8 critical revisions spanning DP specification, IM interface contracts, and safety-critical latency bounds. Given high safety criticality (worksite safety plan integration, SafetyAlert determinism), these revisions must be incorporated into the DP and IM specifications before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. Proceeding to next-level FRs without addressing these revisions would propagate incomplete specifications downstream, creating cascading rework. The revisions are not design flaws but rather necessary formalization and risk mitigation that strengthen the current level before decomposition.

revise.why: DP4 (Hybrid instrumentation and control-system fusion) requires explicit formalization of: (1) transducer data contracts (signal schemas, latency bounds, failure modes) to mitigate Vehicle hardware change diffusion; (2) externalized statistical filtering parameters (outlier thresholds, p95 window size, tuning guidance) to enable site-condition adaptation; (3) phase-inference confidence scoring mechanism to quantify and communicate inference reliability; (4) SafetyAlert latency bounds (≤100 ms) and deterministic fusion algorithms to ensure safety-plan compliance; (5) telemetry rate-limiting strategy to prevent network saturation; (6) pluggable phase-inference module design to support algorithm evolution; (7) sensor health monitoring and degradation detection logic; (8) fallback inference strategies and graceful degradation modes. These formalizations convert DP4 from a logically correct but underspecified design into a complete, safety-critical specification ready for architectural decomposition.

assumptions: Assumption A1: Vehicle control system provides stable, versioned actuator-feedback signals (load confirmation, dump-gate position) via Network Link. If Vehicle firmware changes, NGHE Solution can adapt through independent algorithm versioning and recalibration.

Assumption A2: Transducer hardware (load cell, position sensor) remains calibrated and functional throughout operational life. Sensor degradation is detectable via health-monitoring logic within NGHE Solution.

Assumption A3: Statistical filtering parameters (outlier thresholds, p95 window size) can be externalized and tuned without requiring NGHE Solution code changes. Tasking interface supports dynamic parameter injection.
Assumption A4: Phase-inference logic can achieve deterministic, bounded-latency execution (≤100 ms from phase transition to SafetyAlert emission) on target hardware platform.
Assumption A5: Telemetry network can sustain aggregated phase-event traffic at defined rate limits (e.g., 10 events/sec) without saturation or packet loss.
Assumption A6: Phase-inference algorithm can be encapsulated as a pluggable module, allowing replacement (e.g., with machine-learning models) without affecting core fusion framework.
Assumption A7: Worksite safety plan requirements are compatible with NGHE Solution's SafetyAlert interface contract (latency, determinism, alert taxonomy).
open_risks: Risk R1 (Vehicle Hardware Coupling): Phase-inference logic depends on real-time actuator feedback from Vehicle control system. If Vehicle firmware or actuator interface changes unexpectedly, inference calibration may drift, causing cycle-time measurement errors. Mitigation: Define stable, versioned transducer data contracts; implement adaptive recalibration; version inference algorithms independently.

Risk R2 (Sensor Degradation): Raw transducer signals (load confirmation, dump-gate position) originate from Vehicle hardware. Sensor degradation, calibration drift, or hardware replacement could invalidate fusion assumptions and produce unreliable cycle-time metrics. Mitigation: Implement continuous sensor health monitoring; expose sensor-quality metrics via Telemetry; emit degradation alerts via SafetyAlert.

Risk R3 (Parameter Brittleness): Statistical filtering parameters (outlier thresholds, p95 window size) are currently unspecified. If hardcoded, NGHE Solution becomes brittle to duty-cycle variations or site-condition changes, requiring code changes for tuning. Mitigation: Externalize all filtering parameters via Tasking interface; document parameter sensitivity; provide site-specific tuning guidance.

Risk R4 (Safety-Critical Latency): SafetyAlert interface must guarantee low-latency, deterministic delivery (≤100 ms). If NGHE Solution's fusion pipeline introduces variable latency, safety alerts may be delayed, violating worksite safety plan requirements and creating operational hazards. Mitigation: Implement bounded-latency fusion algorithms; add watchdog timers; define and validate maximum alert-emission latency in SafetyAlert interface contract.

Risk R5 (Telemetry Saturation): Telemetry interface may become a bottleneck if phase-event frequency is high (e.g., 100+ events/cycle). Uncontrolled telemetry traffic could saturate network or overwhelm downstream analytics, causing data loss or latency. Mitigation: Implement event buffering and aggregation in NGHE Solution; define telemetry rate limits; compress or summarize phase events before transmission.

Risk R6 (Inference Failure Modes): Phase-inference logic is a novel algorithmic component. If inference fails (e.g., ambiguous phase transitions, sensor noise, missing actuator feedback), cycle-time computation becomes unreliable or unavailable. Mitigation: Implement inference-confidence scoring; emit low-confidence cycles as anomalies via SafetyAlert; provide fallback inference strategies (e.g., timeout-based phase detection).

Risk R7 (Algorithm Evolution): Phase-inference logic may require updates (e.g., machine-learning models, improved heuristics) as operational experience accumulates. If inference logic is tightly coupled to core fusion framework, updates become risky and costly. Mitigation: Encapsulate phase-inference logic as pluggable module; design framework to support algorithm replacement without affecting core fusion.

Risk R8 (Operational Resilience): NGHE Solution's behavior under degraded conditions (sensor loss, inference failure, network latency) is not yet specified. Operational teams lack guidance on graceful degradation, fallback modes, or manual override procedures. Mitigation: Document fallback inference strategies; specify graceful degradation modes; define manual override procedures in operational procedures.
evidence_needed: Evidence E1: Formal specification of transducer data contracts (load-confirmation and dump-gate-position signals) including signal ranges, update frequencies, latency bounds, failure modes, and versioning strategy. Source: Vehicle control system interface documentation; Network Link protocol specification.

Evidence E2: Specification of statistical filtering parameters (outlier-threshold algorithm, p95-window-size, aggregation strategy) and their sensitivity to duty-cycle and site-condition variations. Source: Duty-cycle analysis; site-condition characterization; parameter sensitivity study.

Evidence E3: Definition of phase-inference confidence scoring mechanism (e.g., 0–100 score based on sensor agreement, actuator-feedback consistency, inference-algorithm confidence). Source: Phase-inference algorithm design; sensor-fusion theory; validation against ground-truth phase logs.

Evidence E4: Latency analysis and validation of NGHE Solution's fusion pipeline, demonstrating that SafetyAlert emission latency is ≤100 ms under worst-case conditions (high phase-event frequency, network congestion, sensor noise). Source: Real-time performance testing; latency profiling; worst-case execution-time (WCET) analysis.

Evidence E5: Telemetry rate-limiting strategy and network capacity analysis, demonstrating that aggregated phase-event traffic does not exceed network bandwidth or overwhelm downstream analytics. Source: Network traffic modeling; telemetry volume estimation; capacity planning study.

Evidence E6: Design specification for pluggable phase-inference module, including interface contracts, algorithm-replacement procedures, and validation framework. Source: Software architecture design; module interface specification; algorithm validation procedures.

Evidence E7: Sensor health-monitoring logic specification, including range checks, rate-of-change limits, cross-sensor consistency checks, and degradation-alert thresholds. Source: Sensor specifications; failure-mode analysis; health-monitoring algorithm design.

Evidence E8: Fallback inference strategies and graceful degradation modes, including timeout-based phase detection, manual override procedures, and operational guidance for degraded-mode operation. Source: Failure-mode analysis; operational procedures; training documentation.

Evidence E9: Validation of DP4 against revised specifications through integration testing, field trials, and safety-plan compliance review. Source: Test results; field-trial data; safety-plan compliance checklist.
